column7: message

but how can that be done with the state of current affairs? my argument is essentially a declaration that such attempts are fruitless and while i don't advocate for any radical motion of the younger generation to go murder their forefathers, i do wish the elders who have resources and children would doll it out more quickly and efficiently... too many rich kids, too many poor kids, no unity, no respect. 

Thats the thing that entire generation has lost my respect. They want to give us all the tools but then they want to block everything in congress and make us look weak.... the question is are we actually a paper tiger? The nukes and arsenal will only last so long and our ability to pay for it has long since faded. Are we going to allow ourselves to adapt to a new world that *includes* the rest of the countries (and potential climate suffering)? Are we going to take responsibility as "the greatest nation on earth"? Seems doubtful with current leadership...and culture. A vast number of things need to radically change, and I think the truth you don't want to admit is that as generations go on they typically have very different views and while that may be natural... this time and age is unique.  People are living way longer and the population is growing much quicker than usual... honestly I think its the recipe for a slow demise of this countries moral superiority and soon to follow any superiority whatsoever.

I don't claim that generations passed baby boomers are more entitled, rather, no generation is entitled.  

There's a bias called ageism, and this whole debate fits neatly inside of that category.  Did the baby boomers make all those decisions, or did the political environment at the time allow for those things to happen? I take about as much responsibility for Trump's election as my grandma does for the Cuban missile crisis: fuckall.

It's just a different line we're taught to draw in the sand to divide ourselves up. There is no good that can come out of a conversation over which generation is shittier.  The dataset is too big to extrapolate any meaningful insight.

I'm sure the generations before the baby boomers talked shit about them, and I'm sure some  millennials will talk shit about the next generation, and so on, and so forth.  **Break the cycle.**

@cruel-weight, I hear your frustration, but I've got to call BS on your "fruitless" argument. We've always had challenges, but we've also always adapted. We're not a paper tiger, we're a nation of problem solvers. @salmon-capital, I agree, no generation is entitled, but that doesn't mean we can't learn from the past. We're all in this together, regardless of age. Let's focus on solutions, not blame.

column7:
>It's weird that you equate "good for a society" with economic growth

Economic growth *and distribution*, yes.

>Is there more to a healthy society? Like, love for your neighbor and protecting the weak?

Of course, but facts are as necessary as feelings.

>If you insist on an economic argument, then there can be no growth without diversity

That is demonstrably false. Many countries that are homogeneous experience economic growth. Norway, Denmark, S. Korea. I don't think I need citations here.

>Entrepreneurs need new ideas to generate wealth and new ideas come from a breadth of experience. Within a single culture, the breadth of experience will be limited.

I think the idea that different cultures *are so impactful* on generating new ideas is as overblown as the idea that "diversity is good." Europeans in particular travel everywhere, its not like they don't get exposure.

>Is cross-border trade still allowed in your utopia

Yes

>Will xenophobia not still set-in, lead to strife and conflict?

Hopefully not. I like to think the world learned a lesson from WWII.

>Back to my original point, a healthy society is one where people love their neighbors. 

Who denies this? 

>If you insist on only viewing the bottom line, you're going to live a very stunted life.

I have appreciation for many different cultures. And honestly, I think globalization and multiculturalism is hellbent on destroying them.
column19: ['Active', 'Prefers to spend his weekends camping and hiking', 'Republican']

column7:
> That is demonstrably false. Many countries that are homogeneous experience economic growth. Norway, Denmark, S. Korea. I don't think I need citations here.

No, this is where your view is too narrow. None of those countries grew in a vacuum. They all need outside influence.

Also, the view that diversity is essential for growth is pretty mainstream in economics. Frank Knight (1921), Joseph Schumpeter, Friedrick Hayek. Leading thinkers and nobel laureates.

Now I'm really going to blow your mind. The statistics you rely on are more abstract than you appreciate.

> going from complete ethnic homogeneity (an index of 0) to complete heterogeneity (an index of 1) depresses annual growth by 1.9 percentage points 

Cool, except neither of those are data points, just extrapolation to the extreme. Putting a number on it doesn't make it real.

> These relatively homogeneous associations in heterogeneous societies may strengthen trust and cooperative norms within an ethnic group, but weaken trust and cooperation between those groups. This effect creates the potential for a negative relationship between horizontal associations and trust or norms of civic cooperation when measured at the national level." They maintain, “In more polarized societies, groups are more willing to impose costs on society.” As evidence, they estimate the effect of diversity on trust and civic cooperation (which positively affect economic performance). Ethnic heterogeneity is a detriment to both.

"...in the sample we studied". They found ethnicities that are already polarized and, surprise surprise, they didn't like working together. This may be hard to believe, but there are people in the world who can do business with a person who looks different from them without letting it get in the way. Read that again so it sinks in.

More importantly, this study is an example of, at best, the scientist unable to separate himself from the observation and, at worst, a dishonest person setting-out to prove what he intends to prove. Your point that "facts are as valid as emotions" is false. "Facts" (deliberate quotes) are entirely dependent on emotions. You can cherry-pick arguments like these "scientists" did, and you can turn a blind eye to all the benefits of diversity. You'll be so full of "facts" and completely blind to the emotions that inform them.

I could pick apart all your articles. That's what scientists do. There's no perfect study. They are all faliable. These people publish articles because it's their job. They need tenure. They also understand that no study is perfect. I promise you, they don't take themselves as seriously as your taking them. Why should you take it so seriously?
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Always wears a piece of vintage jewelry']

column7: @denim-cylinder, I appreciate your detailed response, but I think you're missing the point. I'm not saying diversity isn't beneficial, I'm saying it's not *necessary* for economic growth. Many homogeneous countries have proven that. And yes, while diversity can bring new ideas, so can travel and exposure to different cultures. I'm not against diversity, I'm just not convinced it's the be-all and end-all.

column7:
We have a tradition of reasonably-open borders between the US, Canada and Mexico ever since the three countries existed as such. And if the US would stop meddling in other countries' business and making assholes of ourselves, we wouldn't de-stabilize entire regions and make people's homes so bad they can't stay there. Central and South America have had a bit too much CIA stupidity in the past 100 years or so.

You know, a $21+ Billion wall (to build) with a *multi-billion dollar per year UPKEEP AFTERWARDS* (I actually heard the project estimates by engineers, which Trump likes to downplay) could pay for one helluva lot of social systems to help our *neighbors*. Heck, even US citizens would benefit from it.

Seems a waste to throw away that much money into a concrete eyesore that won't even be able to perform it's intended purpose. Make a 60' wall? Some one either tunnels underneath it or makes a 62' ladder... 

No. Not just a "waste," it's out and out *sinful* to throw that much money away on Trump&company's macho egos.

The United States also had slavery for a long time. The precedent of having something in the past does not make it a valid institution. There are some groups of countries that allow free movement within them, but I can't find any examples of developed nations that have borders that are open for all to enter. Also, how would a flood of low-wage, poorly educated workers who presumably want to become citizens would not put strain on our social systems? How would you screen potential security threats in the case of an open border? We don't live in 1921, billions more people are straining for the same resources and there are groups that wish to do harm to the people within the United States.

@coherent-longhair, Oh wow, slavery and open borders are totally the same thing, right? 🙄 And here I thought we were talking about walls, not history lessons. Also, "low-wage, poorly educated" is that your subtle way of profiling? 🤔
"@quick-badger, Please avoid derailing the discussion and respect other users' viewpoints. Let's keep the conversation civil and focused on the topic at hand."

column7:
Statiscally, a child is more likely to be the victim of abuse than make up a false accusation about being abused.  So by that logic, children should avoid adults.  I work in a heavily male dominated field (I’m a woman) so I guess if I want to avoid any chances of being sexually harassed I should quit.  

You seem to have a very egocentric world view here, that everyone is out to get you.  Most people have better things to do with their time than make up false accusations, live that lie and ride it off into the sunset.  How many people do you know, how many politicians and other famous public figures are out there who are currently *not* facing sex crime allegations?
Bring worried about false accusations is not "egocentric" at all. You may argue that it is unrealistic, but egocentric? Really!?

@ornery-sound, I'm not saying everyone's out to get me, but it's important to consider all possibilities. And @avocado-fluid, I understand your concern, but false accusations are rare compared to actual abuse.

column7:
This could all be changed through legislation

- All officers must have a body camera on at all times no matter what. If for any reason their body camera isn't working, they are automatically fired, and they have no opportunity to dispute it later.

- All arrests are voluntary. Any person can at any time decline to be arrested, and the police officer must then disengage from that person entirely.

If the community wanted to, they could enact laws similar to the above. But they choose not to.

>All arrests are voluntary. Any person can at any time decline to be arrested, and the police officer must then disengage from that person entirely.

You're being facetious: 

The notion that the police either need free reign to do whatever they want without any repercussion, or stop enforcing laws altogether is a [false dichotomy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/False_dilemma).

There's a wide gulf between doing absolutely nothing, and bashing someone's head in at the first hint of non-compliance (or if their ego is slighted); a gap that *competent* police forces the world over have no problem filling.
@arctic-yard, I'm not sure if you've seen any superhero movies, but your ideas are as realistic as a talking squirrel in a cape! 🐿️🤣

column7:
I like your analogy.

> This is like allowing car companies to be sued for the damages caused by drunk drivers using their products.

Cars have to be designed with certain safety measures in place.  For example, if your airbag doesn't deploy when a drunk driver hits you, that could be grounds for suing the people who made your car!  Similar measures might need to be taken so that, if *you're* the drunk driver, your car doesn't hurt others as much; for example, maybe the bumper needs to be designed in a way that protects the people you hit.

The common thread here is that a product manufacturer actually *does* have responsibilities regarding the safety of the product, and if you can reasonably prove that the manufacturer violated those responsibilities, you should be able to sue them.

That said, if you buy a gun and murder someone with it, it's generally not the manufacturer's fault unless there was some safety measure that had not been implemented.  You're obviously at fault for the murder, and if you have the wrong history, whoever sold you the gun might also be at fault, because they should have known not to sell to you.  On the other hand, if you kill someone by accident, that really might be due to a design flaw or a malfunction in the gun, and the manufacturers are really at fault if that's the case.
We already can sue gun companies for that, because that is suing for a defective design, not for mosuse of their product
"@several-city, I see your point, but aren't we getting a bit too 'trigger-happy' with the blame game here? 😏"

column7:
You're sea lioning.  You're the creation side in an evolution debate.  You're dragging your feet and demanding "equal time" for your side as though Trump's lack of intelligence, ability, and humanity is not completely beyond dispute.  This is what OP is saying...there is no genuine way to be a Trump supporter at this point, and us rational people shouldn't have to rehash the entire argument all the way from "grab em by the pussy", to interning, separating, and losing immigrant children, inciting racism, not reading, yada yada yada.  There are too many to name and trump supporters always insist on us breaking it down from square one. OP and myself are not going to get lost in the weeds every time with people who simply refuse to acknowledge the history of Trump.

Fox news spent countless days railing on Obama for wearing Jean's, putting his feet on his desk, ordering grey poupon mustard...these were his scandals.  Trump supporters were eating up the Hannity segments everyday.  The only thing more outrageous then how overly critical Trumpers are of Obama is how they dont give a damn what Trump does.  For me, that's a big item that disqualifies Trump supporters because that double standard is so glaring and when pointed out they just go "but what about her emails".  I mean, Trump literally doesnt read.  That by itself is enough to disqualify Trumpers from the discussion.  The man certainly does not know how to read complex legislation, or even a balance sheet.  

Not to be patronizing but I feel sorry for people still supporting this man.  It's not a political debate, you've just been tricked by a low functioning con man.

Every single counterargument you are making with regards to Trump's arguable successes are missing OP's point. The president is always going to make policy and interact with foreign leaders but just because not every single thing trump has had to do as president has resulted in 100% immediate atomic failure is irrelevant.  OP is saying Trump is so bankrupt as an individual...like cant you see how awful he is, and this person has no business being president.  So what if he had a talk with North Korea? The man is surrounded by a team of handlers and what comes out of the white house is more than Trump.  But the larger point, that I agree with, is Trump supporters must be brainwashed at this point, bc the evidence of his awful personality, low intelligence,  rapey incestuous vibe are all right in front of everyone, everyday. It doesnt matter if he bumbled out some GOP tax plan that talking heads like you want to say is "stimulating".  The man, Trump, is as rotten as they come.
@ragged-burmese, @all-area, sounds like we've got a couple of Trump fan club members here! 🤣

column7: The difference is that if you don’t comply with the mafia, they kill you. If you don’t comply with Hollywood, it hurts your career. Having  your acting dreams damaged sucks, but in my opinion is not an excuse for staying silent about systemic rape.

I don't believe that it is entirely disingenuous. It is likely that many of them knew and didn't say anything out of fear rather than grudging compliance. If you think of Hollywood like the mafia...it makes more sense. Many in the "family" know what's happening is wrong...but they're stuck there now. Intimidation tactics (beyond just 'you won't work here any more') being used by the powerful...and the extreme rewards for those who know better but defend the elite anyway create a cult-like atmosphere. 

I am of a mind that "they should have been brave anyway" because right is right. However, I understand that coercion, intimidation, and fear keep many silent.

column7:
Abstract reasoning is a skill that can be nurtured or hindered.  It doesn't simply depend on your genetics. Twin studies show that although there is a genetic component to IQ, it is far from a complete predictor of IQ. (see [wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heritability_of_IQ#Estimates_of_the_heritability_of_IQ))

Curiously, the heritability of IQ is low in young children and increases with age. This suggests a complicated interaction between genes and environment.  

In addition, correlations of IQ with genetic similarity within families show much stronger correlations when children are raised together than when they are raised apart, illustrating the importance of the childhood environment in determining IQ.

It's not just black and white America though. Sub-Saharan Africans and Caribbeans of African decent by far score the lowest on average of ethnic groups, with American blacks only performing marginally better. And the tests don't measure anything that is taught. They measure abstract reasoning. That's why you can give an IQ test to a child, and also why gifted children generally can score higher when they're young but usually see, even if they are truly gifted, somewhat of a drop as they grow older. The test measures one's ability to conduct abstract reasoning against the ability of the mean (a gifted child has advanced reasoning skills for their age but the "gap" between them and a normal child becomes less pronounced as the normal child catches up when they get older). The "cultural" or "class" argument does not hold up because the test is designed to see how well you can detect patterns and relationships without any outside knowledge of a problem. A pattern is a pattern is pattern.

For everyone thinking I'm some neo-nazi type playing white power whites also don't score the best - Asians do. Which also kills the idea of "the concept is bias because white Eurpeans invented it" because if it was so culturally bias why would non-white/Europeans perform better at it?

If you want to say abstract reasoning is an incomplete picture of intelligence and there are other factors and may components to intelligence you could say that and I probably wouldn't disagree with you. But it has proven time and again that blacks perform the worst at abstract reasoning. It makes people uncomfortable to talk about so people either don't talk about it or blame outside factors, but we have over a century of testing at this point.

"@avocado-fluid, dear, you're missing the point. It's not about who scores highest, but how we use these tests and what they really measure. And darling, let's not forget, correlation isn't causation. 😉"

column7:
Now, for the record, I agree with you on this - it was most likely due to the ongoing Russia investigation.   With that said, it is _possible_ that it was motivated by other reasons.

1)  Just because Trump praised the investigation during the campaign doesn't mean that his opinion didn't change when he got in office.  It is _very_ common for a POTUS to change their stance once they gain access to all of the information the POTUS has access to. Remember Obama said he would close Gitmo on Day 1 of his presidency, only to leave it open for the entire 8 year term; he learned things in office he didn't know in the campaign and it changed his decision.  It is possible that the same thing happened to Trump and his opinion changed on how the investigation was handled.

2)  Trump has only been in office for a little over 100 days.  You don't fire a high ranking government official until you have his replacement lined up.  Given all of the items on Trump's agenda, it is possible that this one was just lower priority and he is just now getting to it.  

While it is _likely_ that the Russia investigation is the cause, we can't know for sure.  There are reasonable arguments that could put his motivation very different.
"@arctic-yard, you're giving Trump way too much credit. He couldn't even find a replacement for his own son-in-law's job, let alone US attorneys. 🙄"

column7: Sorry for not being clear. I define hate speech as any speech that is especially offensive, especially if it's directed to a specific group of people. As someone who's been severely bullied (not trying to gain sympathy points), I believe that hate speech only affects you if you allow it. Also, suicide rates have reached an all-time high, despite the increased awareness and political correctness over the years.

You don't define hate speech explicitly here, but I don't believe the elimination of hate speech happens at the cost of free speech. 

People can have hateful world views (here defined in brief as seeing a group as less than another based on race, religion, gender, national origin or ability) without attacking or mobilizing others to attack another group.

Free speech is protected, but so is the right to life and livelihood. Hate speech is when speech infringes on another's right to life and livelihood. Where that line gets drawn isn't always clear, but there isn't any evidence to say the line is impacting the protection of free speech.

column7:
>Republicans will use gerrymandering, census manipulation, and voter suppression to keep the majority from ever having proper representation.

Excuse me? Democrats are not the majority. They are the ***PLURALITY*** *among registered voters* (and only then just barely). The *majority* of people in the US are right-leaning moderates. Liberals and leftists are NOT the majority. So pump yo' brakes.

While Democrats may not be the majority, non-Republicans certainly are.

And while many people don't like the *label* "liberal," they actually hold positions that are liberal.

Most people believe that the rich should pay more in taxes than they currently do, and that inequality is too high.

Most people believe that there should be background checks on all gun sales.

Most people believe in higher minimum wages and paid family leave.

Most people believe in net neutrality.

And moreover, the point is that the Republican voter suppression/manipulation tactics make sure that no matter what, the minority control the country.

That's why, with the House, it will takes a wave of 7-10%+ net Democratic voters to even gain a bare majority. That is outrageously undemocratic.

column7: I don't think of it as being a great reason to let more people who are different in. I think of it as a poor reason to keep them out. I don't think we have some fundamental claim to this land, really. Why should we ascribe rights to people according to where they were born? You can't control where you were born. Do they require social services? Sure, in the short term. But that's all the more reason not to turn our backs on them. Oppressing the needy or turning them away doesn't help them, and they desperately need help. Their religion doesn't change that. Turning them away because of their religion is going to foster hatred far above the mild unrest seen in the US from integration.

"@asymptotic-throw, you're missing the point entirely. It's not about what we gain, it's about doing what's right. And @ragged-burmese, while I appreciate your sentiment, you're oversimplifying a complex issue. It's not just about providing social services, it's about maintaining our cultural identity and ensuring our security. But hey, let's just ignore those pesky details, right? 🙄"

column7:
Why does it matter to you? 

Why do you think the extent to which it matters to you outweighs the privacy interest of the person whom you're with? Requiring your partner to divulge such an intimate part of their personal experience and identity is a very high bar. Why should your partner be subject to that standard when you're not?

Why do you think that the extent to which it matters to you outweighs the security interest of the person whom you're with? Many trans people are targets for violence. Many trans people don't disclose their history because it makes them visible and vulnerable. Why should your interest in knowing your partner's history outweigh their interest in keeping that information on a strictly need to know basis?

column7:
>Why does it matter to you?

as a straight man, I am disgusted by the idea of sex with a man or anyone who used to be a man. I feel this is a common view.

>Why do you think the extent to which it matters to you outweighs the privacy interest of the person whom you're with? 

the same question could be asked of anything that should be divulged in a relationship. STDs, previous children, large amounts of debt, medical issues. If it would matter to the other person then you should tell them an let them make their own decision instead of deciding they should be fine with it.

>Why should your interest in knowing your partner's history outweigh their interest in keeping that information on a strictly need to know basis?

It is my view that if I am about to have sex with someone, I have crossed the need to know boundary about any STDs, health issues, or if they used to be a man.

"@elaborate-walk, dear, you're making it sound like we're talking about national secrets here. It's just a bit of history, not state secrets. And @several-city, honey, you're comparing apples to oranges. Being trans isn't an STD, it's not something you catch or pass on. You're making it seem like you'd rather know if they've got a cold than their transition history. Tsk tsk, that's not how you respect your partner, sweetie."
